author: Augustine of Hippo
book: A Treatise on the Grace of Christ, and on Original Sin.

classification.cautionReason: No major mainstream heresy warning is attached by the preview classifier.

lineage: A Treatise on the Grace of Christ, and on Original Sin.
title: How Pelagius Deceived His Judges.

verses.book: A Treatise on the Grace of Christ, and on Original Sin.
verses.text: Now, is it by making such statements as these, meeting objections which are urged in one sense with explanations which are meant in another, that he designs to prove to us that he did not deceive those who sat in judgment on him?

verses.book: A Treatise on the Grace of Christ, and on Original Sin.
verses.text: Then he utterly fails in his purpose.

verses.book: A Treatise on the Grace of Christ, and on Original Sin.
verses.text: In proportion to the craftiness of his explanations, was the stealthiness with which he deceived them.

verses.book: A Treatise on the Grace of Christ, and on Original Sin.
verses.text: For, just because they were catholic bishops, when they heard the man pouring out anathemas upon those who maintained that “Adam’s sin was injurious to none but himself, and not to the human race,” they understood him to assert nothing but what the catholic Church has been accustomed to declare, on the ground of which it truly baptizes infants for the remission of sins—not, indeed, sins which they have committed by imitation owing to the example of the first sinner, but sins which they have contracted by their very birth, owing to the corruption of their origin.

verses.book: A Treatise on the Grace of Christ, and on Original Sin.
verses.text: When, again, they heard him anathematizing those who assert that “infants at their birth are in the same state in which Adam was before the transgression,” they supposed him to refer to none others than those persons who “think that infants have derived no sin from Adam, and that they are accordingly in that state that he was in before his sin.”

verses.book: A Treatise on the Grace of Christ, and on Original Sin.
verses.text: For, of course, no other objection would be brought against him than that on which the question turned.

verses.book: A Treatise on the Grace of Christ, and on Original Sin.
verses.text: When, therefore, he so explains the objection as to say that infants are not in the same state that Adam was in before he sinned, simply because they have not yet arrived at the same firmness of mind or body, not because of any propagated fault that has passed on to them, he must be answered thus: “When the objections were laid against you for condemnation, the catholic bishops did not understand them in this sense; therefore, when you condemned them, they believed that you were a catholic.

verses.book: A Treatise on the Grace of Christ, and on Original Sin.
verses.text: That, accordingly, which they supposed you to maintain, deserved to be released from censure; but that which you really maintained was worthy of condemnation.

verses.book: A Treatise on the Grace of Christ, and on Original Sin.
verses.text: It was not you, then, that were acquitted, who held tenets which ought to be condemned; but that opinion was freed from censure which you ought to have held and maintained.

verses.book: A Treatise on the Grace of Christ, and on Original Sin.
verses.text: You could only be supposed to be acquitted by having been believed to entertain opinions worthy to be praised; for your judges could not suppose that you were concealing opinions which merited condemnation.

verses.book: A Treatise on the Grace of Christ, and on Original Sin.
verses.text: Rightly have you been adjudged an accomplice of Cœlestius, in whose opinions you prove yourself to be a sharer.

verses.book: A Treatise on the Grace of Christ, and on Original Sin.
verses.text: And though you kept your books shut during your trial, you published them to the world after it was over.”
